name: T2 35mm Point & Shoot (1990)
description: A luxury compact camera from 1990. Heavy, cold titanium housing one of the sharpest lenses ever put in a pocketable frame.
patina: The titanium has a micro-scratch across the flash housing. The shutter button has polished to a high shine from thousands of presses. The leather case is falling apart, but the mechanics sound like a vault door closing.
whyThisFitsMyArchive: It forces me to slow down. I have 36 shots, and they cost money. I don't take pictures of my food with this. The Carl Zeiss lens captures light in a way algorithms can't replicate. It is a permanent object in a disposable era.
whyIChooseThis: Over an iPhone: The iPhone captures data; the T2 captures memory. Over a Leica: The T2 actually fits in the pocket of my Massimo Dutti coat without destroying the silhouette.
whatItPairsWithInMyCollection: It is always in the left pocket of the Massimo Dutti Coat. It shares the same design philosophy as the Cartier Santos: exposed mechanics, zero apologies.
whatMakesItTimeless: It cannot be updated via firmware. It will never ask to connect to Wi-Fi. It does one thing perfectly, forever.

indianRetailers.name: Local camera stores (Delhi, Mumbai vintage shops)

discontinuedNote: Production ended 2002. Secondary market only. Prices have stabilized at ₹85,000-₹1,10,000 for good condition copies.
alternativeUnder3000: Kodak M35 film camera (~₹2,500 on Amazon India) — plastic body, 35mm, entry-level film
alternativeUnder10000: Olympus Stylus Epic (MJU II) (~₹5,000-₹8,000 used) — 35mm f/2.8 lens, pocketable, much cheaper
premiumAlternative: Leica M6 (~₹5,00,000+ used via mpb.com or local dealers) — the rangefinder benchmark
buyRecommendation: Buy When You Find a Clean Copy — this is a collector piece; condition determines value. Inspect shutter, meter, and lens for fungus before buying. Prices have remained stable at ₹85,000-₹1,10,000 for CLA'd (cleaned, lubricated, adjusted) copies.
